_comment: THE SECTION LIBRARY. Each section TYPE has several design VARIANTS. Same job, different composition. A page is built by picking one variant per section. 'goals' = which goals the variant serves best. 'niches' = ['all'] or specific. 'preview' = the id rendered live in the showcase. 'built' = a production template already exists in src/_includes/sections/. Variants are researched from best-in-class UI/UX, not invented.

items.label: Hero / above the fold
items.purpose: The first thing a visitor sees. States the promise and offers the primary action. 57% of viewing time is spent above the fold.

variants.name: Split hero with action card
variants.description: Headline and copy on one side, a compact booking or quote widget docked on the other.
variants.whenToUse: Hospitality, F&B, clinics: anything where a reservation or enquiry is the primary action.
variants.principle: Puts the conversion action in prime real estate. Embedded booking widgets have lifted direct bookings 40%+ vs linking out.

variants.name: Centered editorial hero
variants.description: One dominant headline, one sub-line, a single CTA, centered over a clean or photographic background.
variants.whenToUse: Personal brands, professional services, premium positioning where message clarity beats features.
variants.principle: One headline plus one CTA reduces hesitation. Clarity beats cleverness for skim-readers.

variants.name: Full-bleed image / video overlay
variants.description: Edge-to-edge background photo or video, headline and CTA overlaid, often with a floating action button.
variants.whenToUse: Hospitality, restaurants, lifestyle where atmosphere sells the experience.
variants.principle: Immersive visuals let visitors picture the experience before they commit; a persistent CTA captures intent throughout the scroll.

items.label: Value propositions / why us
items.purpose: Answers 'why you' fast, inside the first 30 seconds. Designed for scanning, not reading (79% of users scan).

variants.name: Three-pillar benefit grid
variants.description: Three or four icon + short-headline + one-line columns directly under the hero.
variants.whenToUse: Almost any small business that needs to communicate differentiation fast.
variants.principle: Chunked, scannable trios match F-pattern skimming and deliver a 30-second elevator pitch.

variants.name: Editorial numbered rows
variants.description: Benefits as a stacked, numbered editorial list instead of a tile grid.
variants.whenToUse: Brands with an editorial, calmer personality; longer benefit copy.
variants.principle: A vertical numbered rhythm reads as considered and premium, and gives each point room to breathe.

variants.name: Problem-to-outcome split
variants.description: Left side names the pain the customer has; right side shows the outcome you deliver.
variants.whenToUse: Clinics, professional services, B2B-leaning businesses.
variants.principle: Anchoring value to a specific pain and a measurable outcome resonates more than generic feature claims.

items.label: Feature / benefit detail
items.purpose: Explains what is included, in depth, without losing the scanner.

variants.name: Alternating (zigzag) rows
variants.description: Stacked rows alternating image-left/text-right, then text-left/image-right.
variants.whenToUse: Sequential storytelling, services explained step by step, product detail.
variants.principle: The Z-motion mirrors natural scanning and the staggered rhythm sustains attention down a long scroll.

variants.name: Bento grid
variants.description: Modular grid of unequal cards; the largest card is the headline feature, smaller cards are secondary.
variants.whenToUse: Multi-feature offerings shown in parallel: multi-service businesses, shops, SaaS-like.
variants.principle: The largest block claims prime attention while peripheral cards capture the rest. Bento feature sections raised time-on-page ~31% in one study.

variants.name: Feature checklist / spec block
variants.description: Two-column list of capabilities with check icons, optionally beside a single visual.
variants.whenToUse: Clinics and services where 'what's included' must be unambiguous.
variants.principle: Checklists let prospects confirm coverage without endless scrolling, reducing uncertainty before the CTA.

items.label: Social proof / trust
items.purpose: Convinces a skeptic at a glance. Trust cues lift conversions 15-34%.

variants.name: Logo / star-rating trust bar
variants.description: Thin strip of partner or press logos, or an aggregate star rating, placed just under the hero.
variants.whenToUse: Any business; especially shops and clinics needing instant credibility.
variants.principle: Early trust signals establish credibility the moment users land.

variants.name: Testimonial cards / carousel
variants.description: Rotating or grid of customer quotes with photo, name, and a specific result.
variants.whenToUse: Services, personal brands, hospitality.
variants.principle: Specific, attributable stories build trust where generic praise fails. Display a 4.0-4.7 rating, not a perfect 5.0, which reads as fake.

variants.name: Proof under the CTA
variants.description: A short testimonial or customer-count line placed directly beneath a CTA button.
variants.whenToUse: Any conversion-critical CTA or form.
variants.principle: Reassurance at the decision point reduces friction. One company saw a 59% lift placing proof under CTAs.

items.label: Pricing / offer
items.purpose: Presents the offer so the decision is easy and the right option stands out.

variants.name: Three tiers, highlighted middle
variants.description: Three plan cards side by side, the middle one elevated and badged 'Most popular'.
variants.whenToUse: Productized services, memberships, bundles.
variants.principle: Goldilocks anchoring: customers pick the middle option ~65% of the time; highlighting lifts average order value 15-35%.

variants.name: Single offer panel
variants.description: One prominent price or offer block with an inclusions checklist and one CTA.
variants.whenToUse: Personal brands, single-service providers, clinics with one headline package.
variants.principle: One clear offer with a 'what's included' list removes comparison paralysis and focuses the decision.

variants.name: Comparison table
variants.description: Feature rows by plan columns with checkmarks; stacks to accordions on mobile.
variants.whenToUse: Multi-tier offerings where buyers genuinely compare features.
variants.principle: Side-by-side checklists make differences scannable; mobile stacking keeps CTAs visible.

items.label: FAQ
items.purpose: Answers objections and feeds AEO. FAQ markup is ~3.2x more likely to appear in AI answers.

variants.name: Accordion list
variants.description: Vertical stack of collapsible question rows with a caret, full header clickable.
variants.whenToUse: Many short questions: the default for most small-business FAQs.
variants.principle: Collapsing reduces overwhelm and lets users prioritize; the caret matches expectations and aids mobile.

variants.name: Open two-column FAQ
variants.description: All questions and answers visible in a two-column grid, nothing collapsed.
variants.whenToUse: Short FAQs (under ~6) or when uninterrupted reading matters.
variants.principle: NN/G warns against accordions when users need most of the content; hidden content gets forgotten.

items.label: Content / magazine teaser
items.purpose: Owned content that ranks, demonstrates expertise, and feeds AEO.

variants.name: Featured + grid
variants.description: One large featured post plus a grid of smaller cards beside or below it.
variants.whenToUse: Sites with an active blog or news section.
variants.principle: Strong visual hierarchy signals the one to read first, then offers browse options, mirroring editorial curation.

variants.name: Uniform card grid
variants.description: Equal-size thumbnail cards, usually three across, with a 'View all' link.
variants.whenToUse: Quick, low-maintenance 'latest from the blog' teasers.
variants.principle: Equal cards assume parallel browsing: clean and scannable for visitors who jump around.

items.label: Location / contact
items.purpose: Helps people find and reach you, and supports local SEO. 76% who search a local business on mobile visit within 24h.

variants.name: Split: details + map
variants.description: Address, hours and phone in text on one side, a lazy-loaded map on the other.
variants.whenToUse: Single-location local businesses.
variants.principle: A clear map plus text address aids both users and local SEO. Lazy-load the map for Core Web Vitals.

variants.name: Contact card + form
variants.description: Phone, hours, and a short form or 'Book now' placed high, no scrolling needed.
variants.whenToUse: F&B, clinics, walk-in or call-driven businesses.
variants.principle: Highlighting contact above the fold removes the search step; F/Z layout guides the eye to the action.

items.label: Final CTA / conversion strip
items.purpose: The last conversion attempt before exit.

variants.name: Full-width focused banner
variants.description: Edge-to-edge contrasting band, one headline, one button, lots of whitespace.
variants.whenToUse: The closing section of nearly any page.
variants.principle: A single prominent CTA with no competing links is the last attempt before exit; contrast and whitespace draw the eye.

variants.name: Reassurance CTA
variants.description: CTA button paired with a trust line, guarantee, or mini-testimonial directly beneath.
variants.whenToUse: Higher-friction offers: clinics, paid services, checkout entry.
variants.principle: Pairing the ask with proof or a risk-reducer at the decision point cuts hesitation.

items.label: Lead capture block
items.purpose: Turns an anonymous visitor into an owned contact. The job is to not lose the people who were interested but not ready today.

variants.name: Inline one-step capture
variants.description: A short, single-step form (name + email or phone) with a reason line and reassurance microcopy.
variants.whenToUse: Newsletter, guide download, simple enquiry.
variants.principle: Fewest fields wins. Phone is the most abandonment-prone field, so make it optional and say why.

variants.name: Conversational one-question capture
variants.description: Asks one question at a time, chat-style, easiest thing first, phone offered not demanded.
variants.whenToUse: Quote flows and anything past three fields.
variants.principle: One-question-at-a-time capture collects several times more contacts than a static form; each tiny step feels easy.
